audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Ruth famine, migration to Moab, bereavement, return, Ruth's attachment, and Naomi's lament narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for judges-period setting, famine migration, Ephrathite identity, Moabite wives, compounded widowhood, Naomi's return counsel, Orpah's dignified departure, Ruth's attachment oath, Divine-name language, Naomi/Mara wordplay, Shaddai lament, and barley-harvest arrival.

verses.restored: In days when judges judged, there was famine in land. A man from Bethlehem of Judah went to sojourn in fields of Moab—he, his wife, and his two sons.
Name of man was Elimelech, name of his wife Naomi, and names of his two sons Mahlon and Chilion—Ephrathites from Bethlehem of Judah. They came to fields of Moab and were there.
Elimelech, husband of Naomi, died, and she was left with her two sons.
They took for themselves Moabite women as wives. Name of one was Orpah, and name of second Ruth. They dwelt there about ten years.
Both of them also died, Mahlon and Chilion, and the woman was left without her two children and without her husband.
She arose with her daughters-in-law to return from fields of Moab, because she had heard in field of Moab that YHWH had visited his people by giving them bread.
She went out from place where she had been, and her two daughters-in-law with her. They went on road to return to land of Judah.
Naomi said to her two daughters-in-law, Go, return, each woman to house of her mother. May YHWH do covenant-kindness with you, as you have done with the dead and with me.
May YHWH grant you that you find rest, each woman in house of her husband. She kissed them, and they lifted their voices and wept.
They said to her, No, but with you we will return to your people.
Naomi said, Return, my daughters. Why should you go with me? Are there yet sons in my womb who could become husbands for you?
Return, my daughters, go, because I am too old to belong to a husband. Even if I said, There is hope for me—even if tonight I belonged to a husband and also bore sons—

would you wait for them until they grew? Would you shut yourselves away from belonging to husbands? No, my daughters, for it is exceedingly bitter for me on your account, because hand of YHWH has gone out against me.
They lifted their voices and wept again. Orpah kissed her mother-in-law, but Ruth clung to her.
Naomi said, Look, your sister-in-law has returned to her people and to her gods. Return after your sister-in-law.
Ruth said, Do not press me to leave you, to turn back from following you. For where you go, I will go; where you lodge, I will lodge. Your people are my people, and your Divine is my Divine.
Where you die, I will die, and there I will be buried. Thus may YHWH do to me, and thus may he add, if anything but death separates me from you.
When Naomi saw that she was determined to go with her, she ceased speaking to her about it.
The two of them went until they came to Bethlehem. When they came to Bethlehem, all the city stirred because of them, and the women said, Is this Naomi?
She said to them, Do not call me Naomi; call me Mara, because Shaddai has dealt very bitterly with me.
Full I went away, and empty YHWH has brought me back. Why do you call me Naomi, when YHWH has testified against me and Shaddai has brought calamity upon me?
So Naomi returned, and Ruth the Moabite, her daughter-in-law, with her, who returned from fields of Moab. They came to Bethlehem at beginning of barley harvest.

translation_notes: The opening famine makes migration a survival response. Elimelech's family are sojourners in Moab; migration does not make them disloyal, impure, or less worthy.
Ruth and Orpah are repeatedly identified as Moabite women, yet the narrative presents them as loyal family members. Their ethnicity may not be used for xenophobia or anti-intermarriage ideology.
Naomi blesses both daughters-in-law for covenant-kindness toward the dead and toward her. Orpah's return is not betrayal or failed faith; she accepts the future Naomi urges upon her.
Naomi's marriage-and-sons reasoning reflects patriarchal survival structures in which widows had limited economic security. It is not a timeless definition of women's purpose.

Ruth's words are an attachment oath to Naomi that includes people, land, burial, and Divine allegiance. They must not become a formula for coercive conversion, forced assimilation, or severing migrants from prior identity.
Naomi names her bitterness directly and interprets her suffering through YHWH and Shaddai. The narrator preserves her lament without declaring that bereavement proves Divine punishment.
'Empty' describes Naomi's grief and loss, not Ruth's lack of value. Ruth is physically beside her, and the narrative will challenge Naomi's perception of absolute emptiness.
The barley-harvest timing introduces material possibility but does not cancel grief. Recovery begins amid bereavement rather than replacing it.
safety_note: Do not shame migrants, widows, childless people, Moabites, Orpah, or Naomi's lament. Do not preach Ruth's attachment as coerced conversion, romantic self-erasure, or proof that loyalty requires abandoning all boundaries. Preserve grief, chosen kinship, economic vulnerability, and each woman's dignity.
